sentences.text: One morning, the old Water-rat poked his head out of his hole.
His eyes were bright and beady, his whiskers were stiff and grey, and his tail looked like a long piece of black rubber.
The little ducks were swimming in the pond, looking like yellow canaries, while their mother, who was pure white with red legs, tried to teach them to stand on their heads in the water.
"You will never be in the best society unless you can stand on your heads," she kept telling them, and she often showed them how to do it.

sentenceTimings.text: One morning, the old Water-rat poked his head out of his hole.

"You will never be in the best society unless you can stand on your heads," she kept telling them, and she often showed them how to do it.

sentences.text: But the little ducks did not listen to her.
They were so young that they did not understand how important it is to be part of society.
"What disobedient children!" cried the old Water-rat; "they really deserve to be drowned."
"That's not true," replied the Duck, "everyone has to start somewhere, and parents should be patient."

sentences.text: His garden was the most beautiful in the whole countryside, and everyone admired it.

'Have you fixed the hole in the roof yet, little Hans?' cried the Miller in a cheerful voice.
'It is completely fixed,' answered little Hans as he came down the ladder.

sentences.text: 'Ah!' said the Miller, 'there is no work more enjoyable than the work one does for others.'
'It is certainly a great privilege to hear you speak,' answered little Hans, sitting down and wiping his forehead, 'a very great privilege.'
But I am afraid I will never have such beautiful ideas as you have.
'Oh!' replied the Miller, 'you will have wonderful ideas if you keep working hard.'

sentenceTimings.text: 'Ah!' said the Miller, 'there is no work more enjoyable than the work one does for others.'
'It is certainly a great privilege to hear you speak,' answered little Hans, sitting down and wiping his forehead, 'a very great privilege.'
But I am afraid I will never have such beautiful ideas as you have.
'Oh!' replied the Miller, 'you will have wonderful ideas if you keep working hard.'

sentences.text: Little Hans felt happy to hear this, and he was grateful for the Miller's kind words.
He knew that working hard and helping others would bring him joy and good ideas.
The Miller said, 'They will come to you, but you must work harder.'
Right now, you only know how to be a friend, but one day you will understand friendship better.

sentenceTimings.text: Little Hans felt happy to hear this, and he was grateful for the Miller's kind words.
He knew that working hard and helping others would bring him joy and good ideas.
The Miller said, 'They will come to you, but you must work harder.'
Right now, you only know how to be a friend, but one day you will understand friendship better.

sentences.text: Little Hans asked, 'Do you really think I will understand it?'
The Miller replied, 'I am sure you will, but now that you have fixed the roof, you should go home and rest because I need you to take my sheep to the mountain tomorrow.'
Poor little Hans was too scared to say anything, and early the next morning, the Miller brought his sheep to Hans's cottage, and Hans took them to the mountain.
It took Hans the entire day to go there and back, and when he returned, he was so tired that he fell asleep in his chair and did not wake up until it was morning.

Poor little Hans was too scared to say anything, and early the next morning, the Miller brought his sheep to Hans's cottage, and Hans took them to the mountain.
It took Hans the entire day to go there and back, and when he returned, he was so tired that he fell asleep in his chair and did not wake up until it was morning.

sentences.text: Hans said, 'I will have a wonderful time in my garden,' and he started working right away.
However, he could never take care of his flowers because his friend, the Miller, always came by and sent him on long trips or asked for help at the mill.
Little Hans often felt very upset because he worried his flowers would think he had forgotten them, but he comforted himself by remembering that the Miller was his best friend.
Even though Hans was busy helping the Miller, he hoped to spend time in his garden soon.
